enrichment.short_description: File Browser is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application that turns any server directory into personal cloud storage, letting users upload, preview, edit, and manage files through a browser.
enrichment.seo_title: File Browser Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: File Browser review 2026: the open-source, self-hosted web file manager that turns a server into personal cloud storage. Features, setup, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
File Browser (also known by its GitHub organization name filebrowser/filebrowser and its documentation domain filebrowser.org) is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application that provides a browser-based interface for managing files inside a directory on a server. Rather than being a full operating-system file manager, it is scoped deliberately: point it at a folder and it exposes that folder, and only that folder, to authenticated users through a clean web UI, effectively turning any Linux, macOS, Windows, or NAS machine into personal cloud storage without relying on a third-party provider. Core functionality includes uploading and downloading files, creating and deleting folders, previewing images, videos, PDFs, markdown and office-adjacent files, in-browser text and code editing, renaming and moving items via drag-and-drop, and generating shareable links with optional passwords and expiration windows. It ships as a single statically-linked Go binary with an embedded Vue.js and TypeScript frontend, which means deployment is unusually simple: download one executable (or run the official Docker image), point it at a directory, and it starts serving a full file-management interface with no external database server, runtime, or dependency required beyond an embedded BoltDB-based store for users and settings.

The project's history is well documented by its original creator, Henrique Dias (online as 'Hacdias'), a Portuguese software engineer. He began the project as a teenager, at around age 15, building what was first called 'filemanager', an add-on for the Caddy web server, rather than a standalone application. The side project grew unexpectedly: it went viral on GitHub's trending page in 2017, and in Q2 2018 Dias renamed it to File Browser, spun it out of his personal GitHub account into a dedicated filebrowser organization, and moved the primary repository to filebrowser/filebrowser, which is the repository that exists today. The GitHub repository itself shows a creation date of September 11, 2015, reflecting the project's earlier life under its original name and structure. By early 2020, balancing university studies and a job at Protocol Labs left Dias with no time to maintain the growing project, and after failing to recruit a sustained team of active contributors he archived the repository, later handing ownership of the GitHub organization to a volunteer maintainer, GitHub user jeromewu. Dias returned to a more active advisory role several years later after observing signs of neglect, and in a public post in March 2026 he confirmed that File Browser had formally entered 'maintenance-only mode': the maintainers will continue to address bug fixes and security vulnerabilities, but no new features are planned, and feature-oriented pull requests are reviewed only on a case-by-case basis rather than merged as a matter of course. Dias has been candid that the original codebase, written when he lacked professional security experience, has needed ongoing hardening, and that a lesson from the project's growth was that adding features requested by small numbers of vocal users created a maintenance burden disproportionate to its benefit.

Architecturally, File Browser follows a conventional single-binary web-server pattern common to Go tooling. The backend, written in Go, exposes both the web UI and a REST-style API that the bundled frontend consumes; the frontend itself is built with Vue.js and TypeScript and compiled into static assets embedded directly into the Go binary at build time, which is why a single executable can serve a full modern single-page application without a separate web server or build step at runtime. Configuration is intentionally flexible for a self-hosted tool: settings can be supplied through command-line flags, environment variables prefixed with FB_, or a configuration file in JSON, TOML, or YAML format that File Browser automatically discovers as .filebrowser.{json,toml,yaml,yml}. An accompanying command-line interface, invoked as the same filebrowser binary with subcommands, is used to initialize the embedded database, create and manage user accounts, import or export configuration, and toggle individual features. Administrators can define multiple users with distinct home directories, granular permissions on a per-user or per-rule basis (such as read-only access, upload restrictions, or hidden files and folders), and can enable a 'Command Runner' feature that executes shell hooks on events such as uploads, downloads, or logins, useful for triggering backups, transcoding, or notification scripts. The interface supports light and dark themes, custom CSS and logo branding through a dedicated branding folder, image thumbnail generation, and playback of common video formats including MKV directly in the browser. Community translators have localized the interface into more than thirty languages, from Spanish, French, German, Portuguese, Russian, and Chinese to Arabic, Turkish, and Hebrew, making it broadly accessible outside English-speaking markets even though the project itself has no dedicated localization team.

Licensing and pricing are unambiguous: File Browser is distributed under the Apache License 2.0, a permissive open-source license that allows free use, modification, self-hosting, and redistribution, including in commercial contexts, without any license fee. There is no paid tier, no hosted SaaS offering operated by the project, and no enterprise edition; the entire feature set described in its documentation is available to anyone who deploys the binary or Docker image themselves. The only costs a user incurs are their own infrastructure, such as a virtual private server, NAS device, or home server, and the bandwidth or storage that entails. As of mid-2026 the GitHub repository has accumulated roughly 35,600 stars and around 3,950 forks, with 152 tagged releases and an active issue tracker, figures that place it among the most widely adopted self-hosted file-manager projects on GitHub and that reflect the project's long tail of use in home labs, small businesses, NAS add-on ecosystems such as Docker-based NAS communities, and developer utility stacks.

File Browser's target audience is primarily technically comfortable individuals and small teams: homelab enthusiasts running Docker Compose stacks, sysadmins who need a lightweight way to expose a directory to non-technical colleagues without teaching them SFTP, developers who want a quick web front end for build artifacts or logs on a server, and privacy-conscious users who prefer self-hosting over trusting files to Dropbox, Google Drive, or other commercial cloud storage providers. It is generally not aimed at large enterprises needing directory-wide compliance tooling, single-sign-on federation, or multi-petabyte object storage integration out of the box, since those needs sit outside the scope Dias intentionally kept narrow.

Within the self-hosted file-manager category, File Browser competes with tools such as Nextcloud (a much heavier, PHP-based full groupware and sync suite with calendars, contacts, and office collaboration), Seafile (a sync-focused file server with strong deduplication and versioning), Cloudreve and Alist (Go-based alternatives with broader cloud-storage backend integrations), and the PHP-based FileGator, which, like File Browser, focuses narrowly on a browser-based file manager but differentiates itself with built-in multi-cloud storage adapters via Flysystem (S3, FTP, Dropbox, Azure) rather than File Browser's single local-directory model. File Browser differentiates itself primarily through simplicity and deployment friction: a single binary with no runtime dependencies is unusually easy to drop onto a server compared to Nextcloud's PHP, database, and web-server stack, which is a major reason it remains popular for narrowly scoped use cases even without new feature development.

A notable and increasingly important fact about the project's current market position is the emergence of File Browser Quantum, a community fork maintained by developer gtsteffaniak (repository gtsteffaniak/filebrowser) that has become the primary venue for active feature development since the original project entered maintenance mode. Quantum has introduced capabilities the original never had, including support for multiple simultaneous storage sources with include and exclude rules, modern authentication methods such as OpenID Connect (OIDC), LDAP, JSON Web Token-based login, two-factor authentication, and reverse-proxy authentication, an overhauled access-control and sharing system, and tighter integration with OnlyOffice for in-browser document editing. Quantum reached a stable v1.4.0 release in June 2026 and has been adopted by several self-hosting communities and NAS-adjacent platforms as the more actively developed successor, while the original filebrowser/filebrowser project continues to serve as the stable, security-patched baseline that many existing deployments still rely on. This bifurcation, an original project intentionally frozen in scope and a community fork carrying the feature torch forward, is itself a useful signal for anyone evaluating File Browser today: choose the classic project for stability and minimal footprint, or the Quantum fork if features like SSO, multi-source browsing, or OnlyOffice integration are required.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: File Browser is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application that exposes a single directory on a server as a browsable, manageable personal cloud storage system. Users point the tool at a folder, and it serves a clean web interface for uploading, previewing, editing, and organizing the files inside that folder, without requiring a full operating-system-level file manager or third-party cloud account.

It ships as a single compiled Go binary with an embedded Vue.js frontend, which makes installation as simple as downloading one executable or running the official Docker image. There is no separate database server to install, no build step to run, and no paid tier: everything documented on filebrowser.org is available for free under the Apache License 2.0.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The application supports the core file-management operations users expect from a cloud drive: drag-and-drop uploads, downloads, renaming, moving, copying, deleting, and creating new files and folders. It includes an in-browser text and code editor, image and video preview (including MKV playback), and image thumbnail generation for faster browsing of large media directories.

Administrators can create multiple user accounts, each with its own home directory and a granular set of permissions covering uploads, downloads, editing, sharing, and visibility of specific paths. A 'Command Runner' feature lets admins trigger custom shell scripts on events like uploads or logins, useful for automated backups or notifications. The interface can be themed with custom CSS and branding assets, and it is community-translated into more than thirty languages.

long_form_blocks.heading: Architecture and Setup
paragraphs: File Browser configuration is handled through command-line flags, FB_-prefixed environment variables, or an auto-discovered .filebrowser configuration file in JSON, TOML, or YAML format. A companion CLI (the same binary with subcommands) initializes the embedded database and manages users, making it scriptable for infrastructure-as-code deployments.

Because the project entered maintenance-only mode in 2026, with maintainers focused on bug fixes and security patches rather than new features, users who need actively developed capabilities such as single sign-on, multiple storage sources, or OnlyOffice document editing typically look at the community fork File Browser Quantum, which reached a stable v1.4.0 release in June 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: File Browser has no pricing tiers, license fees, or hosted SaaS offering. It is entirely free and open source under the Apache License 2.0, and the full feature set is available to anyone who self-hosts the binary or Docker image. The only cost is the user's own server infrastructure and bandwidth.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tiers or usage limits.
Single-binary, dependency-free deployment makes it one of the fastest self-hosted tools to get running via Docker or a bare-metal executable.
Lightweight resource footprint compared to full sync suites like Nextcloud, making it well suited to small VPS instances or home NAS devices.
Large, mature GitHub community (over 35,000 stars) with community translations into 30-plus languages and extensive third-party deployment guides.
cons: The core project is in maintenance-only mode as of 2026, meaning no new features are being added by the original maintainers, only bug fixes and security patches.

Users who want actively developed features like OIDC/LDAP single sign-on, multiple storage sources, or OnlyOffice integration need to switch to the community fork File Browser Quantum instead.
No official managed hosting option; every deployment is fully self-managed, which requires basic server administration knowledge.

faqs.question: Is File Browser really free?
faqs.answer: Yes. File Browser is fully free and open source under the Apache License 2.0. There is no paid tier, subscription, or hosted service operated by the project; you self-host it on your own server or NAS at no license cost.

faqs.question: What operating systems and platforms does File Browser run on?
faqs.answer: File Browser compiles to a single Go binary available for Linux, macOS, Windows, and ARM-based devices, and an official Docker image is provided, making it straightforward to run on a home server, VPS, or NAS.

faqs.question: Is File Browser still actively developed?
faqs.answer: The original filebrowser/filebrowser project entered maintenance-only mode in 2026, focused on security and bug fixes rather than new features. Active feature development now largely happens in the community fork File Browser Quantum.

faqs.question: Can multiple users have different permissions in File Browser?
faqs.answer: Yes. Administrators can create multiple user accounts, each assigned a home directory and granular permission rules covering uploads, downloads, editing, and visibility of specific files or folders.

faqs.question: Does File Browser support cloud storage backends like S3 or Dropbox?
faqs.answer: The core File Browser project is scoped to a single local directory and does not include built-in cloud storage adapters. Users needing multi-source or cloud-backend support typically use the File Browser Quantum fork instead.

faqs.question: What is File Browser Quantum and how is it different?
faqs.answer: File Browser Quantum is a community fork (maintained by gtsteffaniak) that carries forward active feature development after the original project moved to maintenance-only mode, adding capabilities such as multiple storage sources, OIDC/LDAP/2FA authentication, and OnlyOffice document editing.

enrichment.short_description: FileGator is a free, open-source, self-hosted PHP web application for managing files and folders with multi-user roles, chunked uploads, and support for storage backends like local disk, FTP, SFTP, S3, and Dropbox.
enrichment.seo_title: FileGator Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: FileGator review 2026: a free, open-source, self-hosted PHP file manager with multi-user roles and cloud storage adapters. Features, pricing, alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FileGator is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application for managing files and folders through a browser, built as a decoupled PHP backend paired with a Vue.js single-page frontend. Its GitHub repository, filegator/filegator, was created on June 13, 2019 by developer Milos Stojanovic, and the project has been maintained continuously since, reaching version 7.14.4 by mid-2026 across more than 60 tagged releases. Unlike a simple upload script, FileGator is designed from the ground up as a multi-user file manager: it ships with an admin account and role-based permissions out of the box, letting a server operator create additional user accounts, each of which can be restricted to a specific home folder, given read-only or full read-write access, and organized in a hierarchy where a superuser can view and manage the subfolders belonging to other users. This makes it suitable not just for personal file storage but for scenarios where several people or client accounts need isolated, permissioned access to a shared server.

Technically, FileGator's backend is written in PHP and leans on the popular Flysystem library for filesystem abstraction, which is the architectural decision that gives the tool its most distinctive feature: pluggable storage adapters. Out of the box, and with additional Composer packages where needed, FileGator can be configured to serve files not just from local disk but from FTP and SFTP servers, Amazon S3, DigitalOcean Spaces, Microsoft Azure Blob Storage, Dropbox, and other Flysystem-compatible backends, all through the same unified web interface and permission system. The frontend is a single-page application built with Vue.js, styled with the Bulma CSS framework and Buefy component library, and communicates with the PHP backend over a REST API, which keeps the two halves of the application cleanly separated and makes the backend usable headlessly if desired. File uploads use Resumable.js to provide chunked, pausable, and resumable transfers, which is particularly useful for large files or unreliable network connections, and this chunking can also be used to route around restrictive PHP upload_max_filesize and post_max_size limits that would otherwise block large single-request uploads. The project's test suite uses PHPUnit for functional testing and PHPStan for static analysis, reflecting a reasonably disciplined engineering process for a project of its size.

FileGator is distributed under the MIT License, one of the most permissive open-source licenses available, allowing free use, modification, self-hosting, and redistribution, including in commercial products, without royalties or license fees. There is no paid tier, no hosted SaaS product operated by the FileGator project, and no enterprise edition; every feature documented at filegator.io and in the project's GitHub wiki is available to anyone who deploys the code themselves. A public live demo is hosted so prospective users can try the interface before installing it, but that demo itself is not a commercial offering, just a sandbox instance for evaluation. As of mid-2026 the project has accumulated roughly 3,000 GitHub stars and 443 forks, a smaller but steady following compared to some other self-hosted file managers, reflecting its more specialized focus on multi-user, multi-backend deployments rather than the broadest possible casual audience.

Installation is intentionally lightweight: FileGator requires no database server for its default configuration, since user accounts and configuration can be stored in a JSON file, though it also supports database-backed and WordPress-integrated authentication for installations that need to plug into an existing user system. The setup process documented in FileGator's FAQ involves copying the application files to a PHP-capable web server, ensuring the web server process has read-write permissions on the config and repository folders, logging in with a documented default administrator account, and immediately changing the default password. Because the repository folder is directly served by the web server in a naive setup, FileGator includes an .htaccess rule (on Apache) intended to prevent uploaded scripts from being executed, and it optionally supports URL-action encryption to obscure file operation endpoints from casual inspection; server administrators are nonetheless expected to apply their own hardening, such as running behind Nginx with equivalent protections when Apache's .htaccess mechanism does not apply. Officially supported deployment paths include a Docker image alongside the traditional PHP/Apache or PHP/Nginx stack, which lowers the barrier for teams already comfortable with containerized infrastructure.

The target audience for FileGator skews toward developers, agencies, and IT administrators who need to give multiple external or internal users scoped access to files stored across a mix of local and cloud backends, without building a bespoke access-control layer themselves. Typical use cases include client file exchange portals where each client account only sees their own folder, internal document repositories for small teams, and lightweight replacements for a full groupware suite when the requirement is narrowly 'let named people upload and download files with permissions,' rather than calendaring, contacts, or real-time office document collaboration.

Within the self-hosted file-manager space, FileGator sits alongside tools such as File Browser (a Go-based, single-binary alternative focused on a single local directory rather than multiple storage backends), Nextcloud (a much larger PHP-based groupware and sync suite with calendar, contacts, and office collaboration built in, at the cost of a heavier install and more moving parts), Seafile (a sync-first file server oriented around desktop client synchronization and deduplication rather than a pure browser interface), and Pydio Cells (an enterprise-oriented Go rewrite of an older PHP file-sharing platform with a commercial support tier). FileGator differentiates itself from these primarily through its combination of built-in multi-cloud storage adapters via Flysystem and its role-based, hierarchical multi-user model, delivered in a comparatively small PHP codebase that does not require the database and background-worker infrastructure that Nextcloud or Pydio Cells typically need. Its closest direct comparison in this directory, File Browser, covers similar ground conceptually but takes an intentionally narrower approach, scoped to one local directory with a single Go binary and no native cloud-backend support, which makes the choice between the two largely a question of whether multi-backend storage and hierarchical multi-user roles (favoring FileGator) or single-binary simplicity and minimal footprint (favoring File Browser) matter more for a given deployment.

A few additional facts round out the picture of FileGator's technical evolution and market position. The project supports zipping and unzipping folders directly from the browser, in-app search across the current folder and its subfolder tree, and drag-and-drop uploads with progress and pause/resume controls powered by its chunked-upload implementation. Symlink support exists but is described in the project's own FAQ as unofficial and requiring manual code changes, an honest signal about the boundaries of what is fully supported versus community-patched. Customization is handled through CSS overrides and configuration constants rather than a plugin marketplace, keeping the codebase compact but placing more of the customization burden on the administrator directly editing configuration.php or its sample equivalent. FileGator has also been adopted as a bundled or referenced component inside other self-hosted platforms, appearing for instance as a dependency in projects like HestiaCP hosting control panel forks, which signals a degree of trust from adjacent open-source infrastructure projects even though FileGator itself remains a modestly sized, community-maintained project rather than a venture-backed company with a dedicated support organization.

Product evolution since the 2019 launch has been steady rather than explosive, with more than sixty tagged GitHub releases tracking incremental improvements to storage adapters, upload reliability, and permission handling rather than sweeping redesigns. The choice to decouple the PHP backend from the Vue.js frontend through a REST API has aged well: it means the same backend can, in principle, sit behind alternative frontends or be scripted against directly, and it keeps the codebase easier to reason about for the small group of maintainers and contributors who keep the project moving. FileGator's continued reliance on well-established open-source building blocks, Flysystem for storage abstraction, Resumable.js for chunked uploads, and Bulma and Buefy for UI components, rather than reinventing those layers in-house, reflects a pragmatic engineering philosophy that trades novelty for maintainability, which is a reasonable trade-off for a project maintained largely by volunteers rather than a funded company. For teams evaluating a self-hosted file manager whose primary requirement is flexible storage backend support combined with real multi-user access control, rather than the broader groupware ambitions of Nextcloud or the sync-first design of Seafile, FileGator remains one of the few actively maintained open-source options purpose-built for exactly that niche.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FileGator is a free, open-source, self-hosted web application for managing files and folders, built as a PHP backend paired with a Vue.js single-page frontend. It was created by developer Milos Stojanovic, with its GitHub repository dating back to June 2019, and has since grown into a multi-user, multi-backend file management platform rather than a simple upload script.

The defining architectural choice is its use of the Flysystem library on the backend, which lets FileGator serve files not just from local disk but from FTP, SFTP, Amazon S3, DigitalOcean Spaces, Azure Blob Storage, Dropbox, and other supported storage adapters, all behind a single web interface with role-based permissions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: FileGator supports the standard file operations: copy, move, rename, edit, create, delete, preview, zip, unzip, download, and upload. Uploads are handled with Resumable.js, giving drag-and-drop, chunked, pausable, and resumable transfers that help large files succeed over unreliable connections and work around restrictive PHP upload limits.

Multi-user support is built in from the start, with admin and regular user roles, per-user home folders, and a hierarchy in which a superuser can view and manage subfolders belonging to other users. Authentication can be backed by a simple JSON file, a database, or an existing WordPress user base, and the interface itself is styled with the Bulma CSS framework and Buefy component library.

long_form_blocks.heading: Architecture and Setup
paragraphs: FileGator requires no database server for its default JSON-file configuration, and can run on any standard PHP and Apache or Nginx stack, or via the official Docker image. Setup involves copying the application files to the server, granting the web process read-write access to the config and repository folders, logging in with the documented default administrator account, and immediately changing the default password.

Because the repository folder sits under the web root in a basic install, FileGator includes protections such as an .htaccess rule to block script execution inside uploaded content, and an optional URL-action encryption setting; administrators applying the same protections manually are recommended when deploying behind Nginx.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: FileGator is entirely free and open source under the MIT License, with no paid plans, subscriptions, or hosted commercial offering. A public live demo exists purely for evaluation. All documented features, including every storage adapter and the multi-user permission system, are available to anyone who self-hosts the code at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: Is FileGator free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. FileGator is free and open source under the MIT License, with no paid tiers or subscription fees. You self-host it on your own PHP-capable server at no licensing cost.

faqs.question: What storage backends does FileGator support?
faqs.answer: Through the Flysystem library, FileGator supports local disk storage by default, plus adapters for FTP, SFTP, Amazon S3, DigitalOcean Spaces, Microsoft Azure Blob Storage, Dropbox, and other Flysystem-compatible backends.

faqs.question: Does FileGator require a database?
faqs.answer: No. The default configuration stores users and settings in a JSON file, so FileGator can run on a minimal PHP and web-server stack. Database-backed and WordPress-integrated authentication are also supported for installations that need them.

faqs.question: Can FileGator support multiple users with different permissions?
faqs.answer: Yes. FileGator ships with admin and regular user roles, per-user home folders, and a hierarchy where a superuser can view and manage subfolders belonging to other users, making it suitable for client portals or team environments.

faqs.question: How do uploads work in FileGator?
faqs.answer: Uploads use Resumable.js for drag-and-drop, chunked, pausable, and resumable transfers, which helps large files succeed on unreliable connections and can be used to work around restrictive PHP upload_max_filesize limits.

faqs.question: How is FileGator different from File Browser?
faqs.answer: FileGator is a PHP application built around Flysystem, offering multiple storage backends (S3, FTP, Dropbox, and more) and hierarchical multi-user roles, while File Browser is a single Go binary scoped to one local directory. FileGator suits multi-backend, multi-user deployments; File Browser favors minimal-footprint simplicity.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: FileRun review: pricing, features, self-hosted setup and top alternatives like Nextcloud and ownCloud. See if this private file cloud fits your team in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
FileRun is a self-hosted, web-based file manager designed to give organizations a private, Google Drive-like experience while keeping every file on infrastructure the customer already controls. Rather than shipping as a hosted SaaS product, FileRun is installed on a customer's own Linux or Windows server, VPS, virtual machine, bare-metal box, or NAS device, and it exposes a browser-based interface for browsing, uploading, sharing, previewing, and editing files that already live on that server's filesystem. Because it works directly against existing files rather than requiring a proprietary storage format or a forced data migration, administrators can point FileRun at a folder structure that predates the software and start using it immediately. The product is built and sold by Afian AB, a Swedish company, and its lineage traces back further than the FileRun brand itself: according to FileRun's own changelog page, the company originally launched a file manager called Afian in 2003, reworked that codebase starting in 2006, and sold its first commercial FileRun license on July 17, 2007. A software update system was added in January 2008, and the publicly browsable changelog with per-version detail begins in May 2019, with the vendor noting that records of earlier changes are available on request. This history positions FileRun as one of the longer-running products in the self-hosted file management space, with roughly two decades of continuous development behind the current release line (version numbers now follow a year-based scheme, such as 2026.1.0).

Technically, FileRun is a PHP application that depends on PHP 8.3 or 8.4, the ionCube Loader extension (used to protect the vendor's compiled PHP source, consistent with FileRun's closed-source commercial licensing model), and a MySQL or MariaDB database for storing metadata, settings, and user data. FileRun's own documentation recommends running the database on the same host as the web server, ideally connected over a local socket rather than a network connection, and it lists Libvips, ImageMagick, or GraphicsMagick as requirements for generating thumbnails and image previews. The software communicates with clients over standard HTTP and WebDAV, which is part of why FileRun advertises 'no vendor lock-in' — files remain accessible through generic WebDAV clients even outside FileRun's own interface. On top of this web core, FileRun ships native desktop and mobile clients for Windows, Mac, Linux, iOS, and Android that provide folder synchronization, similar in spirit to Dropbox's or Google Drive's desktop sync agents. For office productivity, FileRun integrates with several external document-editing engines rather than building its own editor: ONLYOFFICE, Collabora Online, and LibreOffice Online for self-hosted editing, plus optional integrations with Google Docs, Microsoft 365, and Zoho Docs for teams that prefer cloud-hosted editors. Authentication can be delegated to LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, Authentik, or WordPress, which makes FileRun straightforward to slot into an existing corporate identity system rather than maintaining a fully separate user directory.

FileRun's pricing is structured around four published tiers, all listed in euros and billed either as a one-time lifetime fee or an annual per-user subscription. The Family plan costs EUR99 as a one-time payment and grants a lifetime license with free software updates for up to 5 user accounts (with one additional account granted per year), but it is explicitly restricted to non-commercial use — FileRun's own terms state it is 'not for work, freelancing or family businesses' — and includes a 14-day money-back guarantee along with community forum access. The three business tiers are all priced at EUR10 per user per year, billed annually, but differ in scale and support: the Small Business plan covers 10 or 20 users with installation-assistance-only support and a 30-day money-back guarantee; the Medium Business plan covers 30 or more users and adds email support with a maximum 24-hour response time for critical issues and 36 hours for non-critical issues (Monday-Friday, 9-to-5), plus a free Docker-based installation service; and the Large Business plan covers 100 or more users, grants nine additional free guest accounts for every paid user account, and includes premium support with 24-hour response times that extend to weekends and holidays, along with a free, fully managed server installation and configuration service that can include desktop-sharing troubleshooting. This tiering means FileRun's real cost scales with headcount rather than storage volume, which is a meaningfully different pricing axis than consumer cloud storage services that charge per gigabyte.

FileRun's target audience is squarely small and medium-sized businesses, IT administrators, and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a polished, Drive-like file-sharing experience without handing data to a third-party cloud provider. Its documentation and marketing lean heavily on NAS compatibility — the vendor explicitly calls out Unraid and TrueNAS support alongside general Docker deployment — which has made it popular in home-lab and prosumer self-hosting communities that already run a NAS and want a nicer front end than raw file shares. On the higher end, FileRun's white-label capability, which allows companies to publish their own branded desktop and mobile apps and customize logos, color themes, and system messaging, positions it as an option for agencies or software resellers that want to offer file-sharing portals to their own clients under their own brand. In third-party software comparison sites, FileRun is most frequently benchmarked against Nextcloud, ownCloud (including its newer Infinite Scale/OCIS engine), Seafile, FileCloud, and, at the consumer end, Dropbox and Google Drive themselves. Reviewers commonly note that FileRun scores well specifically on file-sharing usability and mobile app quality, and that its support responsiveness is often rated favorably, but that it is a narrower, single-purpose file-management product compared to Nextcloud's broader digital-workplace suite, which bundles calendars, contacts, chat, and video conferencing alongside file storage. Users who need that broader groupware functionality tend to choose Nextcloud instead, while users who specifically want a lightweight, fast, Drive-like interface with minimal server overhead tend to favor FileRun, particularly on older or resource-constrained hardware, which FileRun's marketing specifically claims to support well.

Among FileRun's more distinctive, verifiable facts: its interface has full (100 percent) translation coverage in 35 languages, spanning everything from French, German, and Chinese to less commonly localized languages like Albanian and Bengali, which is unusually broad localization for a self-hosted tool of its size. The vendor also warns customers against downloading unofficial translation files from third-party sites, since such files can contain malicious PHP code — a reflection of FileRun's PHP-based architecture and the security risks inherent in loosely distributed community translation packages for that platform. FileRun's plugin and customization system extends beyond translations to authentication providers, document editors, and full white-label app publishing, giving it more built-in extensibility than a purely fixed-feature commercial product, even though it remains closed-source rather than open-source software. Because the software is not open source, prospective customers cannot audit or self-patch the codebase the way they could with a GPL or AGPL project, which is a meaningful consideration for security-conscious buyers comparing FileRun against open-source alternatives like Nextcloud or Seafile Community Edition. Overall, FileRun's market position is that of a long-running, narrowly focused, commercially licensed private-cloud file manager: mature enough to have nearly two decades of development history behind it, priced accessibly on a per-user basis for small teams, and differentiated primarily by its polished Drive-like UI, deep office-suite integrations, and willingness to run comfortably on modest self-hosted or NAS infrastructure rather than requiring a heavyweight server stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: FileRun is a self-hosted file manager that gives businesses a private, Google Drive-style interface for storing, sharing, and collaborating on files that live entirely on a server the customer controls. It is installed directly onto existing infrastructure — a Linux or Windows server, a VPS, a virtual machine, bare metal, or a NAS device such as Synology, Unraid, or TrueNAS — and works with files already present on that server's filesystem, so there is no forced migration into a proprietary storage format.

The product is developed by Afian AB and traces its roots to a file manager called Afian first launched in 2003, reworked from 2006 onward, with the first commercial FileRun license sold on July 17, 2007. That long development history has produced a mature, PHP-based application that now follows a year-based release numbering scheme.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: FileRun combines a browser-based file manager with native desktop sync clients for Windows, Mac, and Linux, and mobile apps for iOS and Android, giving it feature parity with consumer cloud-sync tools while keeping data on-premises. It integrates with ONLYOFFICE, Collabora Online, LibreOffice Online, Google Docs, Microsoft 365, and Zoho Docs for in-browser document editing, and supports authentication via LDAP, Active Directory, OAuth2, Authentik, and WordPress for enterprise identity integration.

Administrative features include white-label branding with custom logos and color themes, publishable white-label desktop and mobile apps, file versioning, resumable uploads, unlimited file sizes, and standard WebDAV/HTTP access that avoids vendor lock-in. The interface has full translation coverage in 35 languages, and the underlying stack (PHP 8.3/8.4, MySQL/MariaDB, ionCube Loader, and Libvips/ImageMagick for thumbnails) is documented in detail on FileRun's own support site.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: FileRun sells four tiers, all denominated in euros. The Family plan is a EUR99 one-time lifetime license for up to 5 non-commercial user accounts (plus one added per year), while the three Business plans are each priced at EUR10 per user per year, billed annually, and scale from Small Business (10-20 users, installation-assistance support) through Medium Business (30+ users, weekday email support and free Docker installation) up to Large Business (100+ users, nine free guest accounts per paid seat, and free premium installation with weekend/holiday support).

Because pricing scales per user rather than per gigabyte, FileRun's cost structure is closer to a traditional software license than to consumer cloud storage. The Family plan carries a 14-day money-back guarantee and the Business plans carry a 30-day guarantee, and support scope — from self-service to fully managed installation — expands materially at the higher tiers.

enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Filestash is an open-source, self-hostable universal file management platform that gives users a single web interface, API, and set of protocol gateways for interacting with data spread across dozens of different storage backends and protocols. Rather than being a storage service itself, Filestash is a client and gateway layer: it connects to existing storage such as Amazon S3 and S3-compatible object stores, SFTP, FTP, WebDAV, SMB, NFS, IPFS, Azure, and SharePoint, plus roughly 25 total supported backends, and presents them all through one consistent Dropbox-like browsing experience. The project's GitHub repository, maintained by developer Mickael Kerjean under the account mickael-kerjean, was created on June 13, 2017, and the software was previously known as Nuage before being rebranded to Filestash. Its source code is published under the AGPL-3.0 license and, as of mid-2026, the repository has accumulated roughly 14,400 GitHub stars and nearly 1,000 forks, with more than 2,400 commits — a substantial open-source footprint for a niche infrastructure category. The codebase is written primarily in Go (roughly 51 percent of the code) with JavaScript (about 35 percent) powering the front end, alongside smaller amounts of CSS, an RPC layer, C, and HTML, reflecting a Go backend serving a JavaScript single-page-application frontend.

Filestash's defining technical characteristic is its plugin-driven architecture, which the project describes with the philosophy that 'everything that matters is a plugin.' The core is kept intentionally minimal and opinion-free, while storage backends, authentication mechanisms, file previewers, and workflow behaviors are implemented as swappable plugins. This design lets Filestash support an unusually broad range of specialized file viewers — the project lists support for photography, astronomy, GIS, 3D, and biomedical file formats among its app integrations — without bloating the core engine. Beyond the browser UI, Filestash exposes 'universal access' through multiple surfaces simultaneously: a web client, a general API, and protocol gateways that let other tools talk to Filestash-managed storage over SFTP, S3, FTP, WebDAV, the Model Context Protocol (MCP), and AS2. This gateway model is notable because it means Filestash can act as a translation layer — for example, exposing an SMB share as an S3-compatible endpoint, or an SFTP server as a WebDAV mount — which is a more infrastructure-oriented capability than most consumer-facing file-sync products offer. Additional platform features include a workflow engine for automating actions in response to file events, immutable and tamper-proof audit logging aimed at GDPR and broader compliance requirements, role-based access control (RBAC), quota management, antivirus scanning integration, file versioning, and multiple selectable UI themes. More recently, Filestash has added AI-oriented capabilities, including semantic search, smart folders, and OCR, with the option to run supporting models locally via Ollama rather than sending data to a third-party AI provider — a detail consistent with the project's broader emphasis on data sovereignty and self-hosting.

Filestash's pricing follows a three-tier structure published on its official pricing page. The Hobby tier is free and open source, self-hosted under the AGPL-3.0 license, and capped at up to 3 users, making it suitable for individuals or very small teams who are comfortable running and maintaining the software themselves with community support. The Self Served (also referred to as Standard) tier starts at $50 per month, billed monthly, and unlocks a commercial license along with enterprise-style authentication — SAML, OIDC, and LDAP single sign-on with role-based access control — for up to 3 seats on a single-node deployment, available either self-hosted or as a managed cloud deployment, with a free trial offered. The Enterprise tier starts at $290 per month, with the company advertising flexible plans for larger organizations that can extend to $750 per month and beyond depending on requirements; Enterprise unlocks unlimited users, audit and compliance tooling, white-labeling, 24/7 support, high-availability infrastructure, a dedicated account manager, custom SLAs, managed upgrades, and professional and security services, including the development of custom plugins for enterprise customers. Both self-hosted and SaaS (cloud-hosted) deployment are offered at the same price points, and billing runs through Stripe with month-to-month cancellation. This pricing structure — a genuinely free and unlimited-feature-but-user-capped open-source tier, a mid-tier aimed at small teams needing basic SSO, and a much higher-priced enterprise tier for compliance-heavy organizations — mirrors the classic open-core commercial model used by many self-hosted infrastructure projects.

Filestash's target users span two fairly different groups. On one end are individual developers, homelab operators, and small technical teams who self-host the free Hobby edition to get a clean web UI for browsing an S3 bucket, SFTP server, or NAS share without paying anything, relying on the AGPL-3.0 license and community support (IRC and Open Collective donations) rather than a commercial contract. On the other end, Filestash markets directly to large enterprises and public-sector organizations that need managed file transfer (MFT), secure multi-backend file sharing, and compliance-grade audit trails; the company's own marketing names customers including MIT, JP Morgan, Bell, Orange, UCI, Monash University, Schneider Electric, and government bodies including the European Commission and Ukraine, along with specific deployment examples such as MIT combining SMB, LDAP, and Duo two-factor authentication, and UCI using Filestash for a campus data cluster. This split positioning — free and open for hobbyists, expensive and feature-rich for regulated enterprises — is a common pattern in commercial open source but is executed with unusually granular technical depth in Filestash's case, given its protocol-gateway and plugin capabilities.

In the competitive landscape, Filestash is most often compared to other self-hosted file-management and sync platforms such as Nextcloud, ownCloud, and FileRun, as well as to dedicated protocol clients like WinSCP or Cyberduck and managed S3 browser tools, though Filestash differentiates itself by acting as a true multi-protocol gateway rather than a single-storage-backend sync tool. Where Nextcloud and ownCloud are built primarily around their own internal storage and a broader groupware feature set (calendars, contacts, chat), and FileRun is built around managing files that already sit on the host server's own filesystem, Filestash's core value proposition is connecting to storage that already exists elsewhere — S3 buckets, existing FTP/SFTP servers, SMB shares, and more — and unifying access to all of it through one interface, one set of access controls, and one audit trail, without requiring data to be copied or migrated into a new system first. This makes Filestash particularly relevant for organizations with heterogeneous legacy storage (a mix of on-premises file servers, cloud object storage, and third-party SFTP endpoints) that need a single governance and access layer spanning all of it, rather than organizations looking to replace their storage entirely with a new all-in-one cloud-storage platform. Notable, verifiable facts about the project include its AGPL-3.0 open-source license (a stronger copyleft license than the plain GPL, which requires that modified versions run as a network service also release their source), its origin as a rebrand of an earlier project called Nuage, its roughly 14,400 GitHub stars as of mid-2026, its Go-and-JavaScript technology stack, and its support for the Model Context Protocol as one of its native gateway options — a modern addition that reflects the project's ongoing evolution to stay relevant as AI agents and tools increasingly need programmatic access to file storage.

It does not require migrating data into a new proprietary store; instead it acts as a unified access and governance layer on top of storage that already exists.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Filevine review 2026: an in-depth look at this legal operating system's LOIS AI, case management tools, custom pricing, and top alternatives for law firms.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Filevine is a cloud-based legal operating system built for law firms, government legal departments, and in-house legal teams, combining case and matter management, document handling, intake, depositions, e-signatures, and business analytics into a single platform. At the center of the modern product is LOIS, short for Legal Operating Intelligence System, an AI layer that the company describes as working across many matters simultaneously rather than being confined to individual tasks. Filevine positions LOIS as capable of both reading and writing to a firm's data, meaning the AI can act on matters, such as drafting motions, summarizing depositions, or surfacing case insights, rather than only summarizing existing content. The company states this AI layer is built on a structured legal knowledge graph drawn from more than 40 million matters processed on the platform, which it uses to ground AI outputs in real case data rather than generic legal text.

Filevine was founded in 2014 and was originally headquartered in Las Vegas, Nevada, before relocating its headquarters to Salt Lake City, Utah, around 2020, where it remains based today. The company grew from a case management startup into one of the best-capitalized companies in legal technology. According to Crunchbase, Filevine has raised roughly $548 million in total funding across eleven rounds since its first outside financing in 2017. The largest and most consequential round to date was a $400 million Series E completed in September 2025, co-led by Insight Partners, Accel, and The Halo Fund, with participation from earlier backers including Meritech, StepStone Group, Signal Peak, Run Ventures, and Album Ventures. That round pushed Filevine's valuation to an estimated $3 billion, making it one of the highest-valued companies in the legal technology sector and, according to Crunchbase News coverage of the deal, part of an all-time high wave of venture investment into AI-driven legal tech. Filevine has used successive funding rounds to expand from a single case-management product into a broader suite covering intake, depositions, contract and document assembly, and firm-wide AI agents, while also growing its headcount into the hundreds of employees serving several thousand law firm and legal-team customers.

Technically, Filevine operates as a multi-tenant SaaS platform organized around discrete product modules that firms can combine: Matters (the core case management engine, covering tasks, deadlines, notes, and collaboration), Intake (lead capture and case evaluation workflows), Depositions (transcript and testimony management), Signatures (native e-signature collection), and document assembly and contract management tools that generate filings and agreements from templated data fields tied to a matter. LOIS sits above these modules as a conversational and agentic AI interface, letting users ask questions from anywhere in the platform and receive answers, drafts, or summaries pulled from the underlying legal graph. Filevine also exposes a public, documented REST API (the Filevine API Gateway) that lets developers automate actions and connect Filevine to outside systems, and it ships native mobile apps for iOS and Android so attorneys and staff can access cases, documents, and tasks away from their desks. Pre-built integrations extend the platform into accounting and payments, including QuickBooks Online, Xero, and LawPay, plus legal-specific tools such as InfoTrack, OurFamilyWizard, Case Status, ChartSquad, and Legl, allowing firms to keep billing, trust accounting, and case data in sync without manual re-entry.

Filevine does not publish self-serve pricing tiers. Its pricing page states plainly that packages are custom-built for each firm's needs organized around the product modules a customer selects (LOIS, Matters, Intake, Depositions, Signatures), and it directs prospective buyers to book a demo, start a free trial, or contact the sales team for a quote. This sales-led, quote-based pricing model is standard among enterprise legal case-management vendors, whose costs typically scale with attorney seat count, matter volume, and which modules are licensed. Filevine does offer a free trial period so firms can evaluate the platform before committing to a contract.

The platform's target users span the full range of firm sizes, from solo practitioners to Big Law, but it has built particularly deep functionality for high-volume plaintiff-side practice areas: personal injury, mass torts, family law, immigration, insurance defense, criminal defense, and estate planning. It also serves government legal functions, including prosecutors' offices, public defenders, and state, local, and federal agencies. Publicly referenced customers include FedEx's legal department, the law firm Skadden, Utah County Public Defenders, and Singleton Schreiber LLP, illustrating a customer base that spans large corporate legal departments, elite law firms, government offices, and plaintiff-side practices alike.

Filevine competes most directly with Clio, MyCase, Smokeball, PracticePanther, and CARET Legal in the broader legal practice-management category. Its differentiation centers on the LOIS AI layer being deeply embedded across the entire platform rather than bolted on as a standalone chat feature, and on its firm-wide approach to automation, which the company markets as scaling every lawyer in a firm rather than a single power user. Review aggregators reflect strong customer sentiment: Filevine holds roughly a 4.8 out of 5 rating on G2 across more than 300 reviews and about 4.6 out of 5 on Capterra across roughly 286 reviews, with reviewers frequently citing document management, workflow automation, and reporting as standout strengths, alongside a note that support quality feedback is mixed across sources. The company has also published customer outcome statistics tied to LOIS, citing figures such as 15 percent increased output, 30 percent faster case recall, and more than 14 percent reduced drafting time for firms using the AI tools.

Architecturally, Filevine's evolution illustrates a broader trend in legal technology: moving from siloed, task-specific software toward a unified operating system model where case data, documents, communications, and AI reasoning all live in one connected graph. Rather than treating AI as a separate add-on module, Filevine has structured its knowledge graph so that LOIS can query and act across intake, matters, depositions, and signatures simultaneously, which the company argues produces more consistent and higher-context outputs than point-solution legal AI tools that only see a single document or case file at a time. This firm-wide graph approach, combined with an open API and native mobile apps, positions Filevine as infrastructure for a law firm's daily operations rather than a bolt-on productivity tool, which is consistent with its recent fundraising being framed by investors and press coverage as a bet on AI-native legal operating systems rather than incremental case-management software upgrades.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Filevine is a cloud-based legal operating system that combines case and matter management, intake, depositions, e-signatures, and document assembly into a single platform for law firms, government legal offices, and corporate legal departments.

Founded in 2014 and now headquartered in Salt Lake City, Utah, Filevine has grown into one of the most heavily funded companies in legal technology, having raised roughly $548 million in total venture funding and reaching an estimated $3 billion valuation following a $400 million Series E round in September 2025.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the core of Filevine's modern platform is LOIS (Legal Operating Intelligence System), an AI layer built on a legal knowledge graph drawn from more than 40 million matters, capable of both reading case data and writing directly into a matter, such as drafting motions or summarizing depositions.

Beyond AI, Filevine offers dedicated modules for Matters, Intake, Depositions, and Signatures, a documented public API for custom automations, native iOS and Android mobile apps, and pre-built integrations with accounting and legal tools including QuickBooks Online, Xero, LawPay, and InfoTrack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Filevine does not publish fixed pricing tiers. Instead, every package is custom-built based on the modules a firm selects (LOIS, Matters, Intake, Depositions, Signatures) and the number of seats required, with pricing determined through a sales consultation.

Prospective customers can start a free trial or book a demo before requesting a formal quote, which is standard practice among enterprise legal case-management vendors whose costs scale with firm size and licensed modules.

faqs.question: What is Filevine?
faqs.answer: Filevine is a cloud-based legal operating system for law firms and legal teams that combines case management, intake, depositions, e-signatures, document assembly, and an AI layer called LOIS into one platform.

faqs.question: How much does Filevine cost?
faqs.answer: Filevine does not publish fixed pricing. Packages are custom-built based on the modules and seats a firm needs, and pricing is provided through a sales quote after a demo or free trial.

faqs.question: What is LOIS in Filevine?
faqs.answer: LOIS (Legal Operating Intelligence System) is Filevine's AI layer, built on a legal knowledge graph covering more than 40 million matters, that can draft documents, summarize case data, and act across a firm's matters rather than a single file.

faqs.question: Does Filevine have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes. Filevine offers native mobile apps for both iOS and Android so attorneys and staff can access cases, documents, and tasks remotely.

faqs.question: Who is Filevine best suited for?
faqs.answer: Filevine serves solo practitioners through Big Law firms, with particularly deep functionality for personal injury, mass tort, family law, immigration, insurance defense, criminal defense, and estate planning practices, as well as government legal offices.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Filevine?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Clio, MyCase, Smokeball, PracticePanther, and CARET Legal, all of which compete in the legal practice management category.

enrichment.seo_title: Fillout Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Fillout review 2026: explore this no-code form and survey builder's Notion and Airtable sync, free plan, paid pricing tiers, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Fillout is a no-code online form builder used to create forms, surveys, quizzes, and lead-capture pages that can be embedded on websites or shared as standalone links. The product is built around a drag-and-drop editor with more than 50 field types, conditional logic and branching, payment collection, scheduling and calendar booking, PDF generation from submissions, and native e-signature collection, positioning it as a broader 'forms that do it all' platform rather than a single-purpose survey tool. Fillout markets itself directly against Typeform, Jotform, Google Forms, and Airtable Forms, arguing that its native, two-way data integrations differentiate it from competitors that require middleware like Zapier or Make to connect a form to a database.

Fillout was founded in 2022 by Antony Toron and Dominic Whyte. According to the company's own about page, the founding team includes engineers, marketers, and product specialists who previously worked at Microsoft, Retool, and Jet.com, and the company describes itself as a deliberately lean team focused on shipping high-quality features quickly. Since launch, Fillout has grown to serve more than 100,000 organizations and millions of forms, and had surpassed 10,000 paying customers by the time of a significant corporate change in 2025 and 2026: the company restructured under a new parent brand called Zite. Under this restructuring, Fillout continues to operate as its own product at fillout.com with existing accounts, forms, and branding intact, while Zite becomes the umbrella company name and also houses a newer, separate AI-powered product (also called Zite, launched in April 2025) that lets non-engineers build custom business applications, plus a shared 'Zite Database' storage layer used across both products. The company has framed this change as expanding what Fillout can do, for example by sharing permissions, access controls, and database infrastructure with the new Zite application-building product, rather than discontinuing the Fillout brand that its existing customer base already relies on.

Technically, Fillout's most distinctive capability is native, two-way synchronization with Notion and Airtable: when a form is submitted, Fillout can create or update a record directly in a connected Notion database or Airtable base without relying on Zapier, webhooks, or other middleware, which the company positions as removing latency, extra subscription costs, and sync failures common with automation-platform-based integrations. Beyond Notion and Airtable, Fillout offers more than 30 native integrations spanning CRM, payment, and marketing tools, alongside an AI form generator that can create a working form from a natural-language prompt. Forms can be customized with custom themes, fonts, favicons, and CSS on higher tiers, embedded inline on external websites, or hosted on a custom domain. Every plan, including the free tier, includes unlimited seats and unlimited forms, a notable departure from many SaaS tools that charge per seat; the primary constraint that scales with price is the number of monthly form responses a workspace can collect.

Fillout's pricing page lists five tiers. The Free plan costs $0 and includes 1,000 responses per month, unlimited forms, unlimited seats, unlimited questions, and access to all 50-plus field types along with core functionality like embedding, payments, scheduling, PDF generation, conditional logic, file uploads, and workflows. The Starter plan costs $15 per month when billed annually ($180 per year, versus $228 if paid month-to-month at $19/month), raises the response cap to 2,000 per month, and adds features such as all question types, custom endings, login forms, redirect-on-completion, CAPTCHA, premium field types, custom themes, a custom logo, Calendly scheduling, and signature collection. The Pro plan costs $40 per month billed annually ($480 per year, versus $588 billed monthly at $49/month), raises the response cap to 5,000 per month, and adds custom emails, removal of the 'Powered by Fillout' branding, custom share links, custom fonts and favicon, and form preview customization. The Business plan costs $75 per month billed annually ($900 per year, versus $1,068 billed monthly at $89/month), removes the response cap entirely for unlimited responses, and adds form analytics with conversion tracking, a custom domain, custom code injection, partial submission capture, data pre-fetching, priority support, and larger file upload limits of 1GB or more. Above these self-serve tiers sits an Enterprise plan with custom, quote-based pricing that adds single sign-on (SSO), support for database records exceeding one million rows, unlimited automated workflow runs, audit logs, dedicated support, and custom service-level agreements.

Fillout's target users are primarily no-code builders, operations and marketing teams, agencies, and small-to-midsize businesses that need forms connected directly to a database or CRM without building custom automation pipelines. Its unlimited-seats model on every tier, including free, makes it particularly attractive to teams that want to give broad access to non-technical staff without per-seat costs, and its native Notion and Airtable sync makes it a natural fit for teams already standardized on those tools for internal data management.

In the competitive form-builder market, Fillout is most frequently compared to Typeform, known for polished, conversational-style forms but reliant on Zapier or Make for database integrations; Jotform, a long-established player since 2006 with a very broad feature set that Fillout claims near feature parity with on most form-building capabilities; Google Forms, a free but comparatively basic option; and Airtable Forms, a native but limited form feature within Airtable itself. Fillout's differentiation rests on combining a generous free tier, unlimited seats and forms across all price points, and native two-way integrations that avoid the latency and cost of automation middleware, an argument the company reinforces through a series of published head-to-head comparison pages against each of these competitors.

Architecturally, Fillout is a modern cloud-based web application rather than a self-hosted or open-source product, and it has steadily expanded from a pure form-and-survey builder into a wider workflow tool encompassing scheduling, payments, e-signatures, and PDF generation, converging toward the same 'operations platform' territory that competitors like Jotform and Typeform have also moved into. Its most recent evolution, the 2025-2026 restructuring under the Zite parent brand, reflects an attempt to extend beyond forms into full custom business-application building while explicitly preserving the standalone Fillout product and brand that its existing customer base already trusts, rather than forcing a disruptive rename of the product itself.
